findings.title: Reported Wasserstein distances have no producing code; repo computes L1 instead
findings.quote:
Grep for 'wasserstein' anywhere in repo (case-insensitive):
  hits (excluding .git): 0
findings.claim: The paper reports an average Wasserstein distance (p=1) between embedding and interpretation prediction distributions (SiMEC median 0.0, SiMExp median 0.049), but no Wasserstein computation exists in the repo; the notebook only computes an L1/cityblock distance (`safe_cityblock`, cells 18/21) saved to tables/decoding_difference.csv.
findings.concern: The specific metric and numbers reported for objective (ii) (embedding-vs-interpretation consistency) are not reproducible from the released code, and the released code computes a different distance than the one the paper names.
findings.resolution: Authors: provide the script that computes the Wasserstein (p=1) distances quoted in Section 4, or confirm whether the L1 'decoding_difference' in the notebook is what was actually reported under the name Wasserstein.
findings.paper_ref: Section 4, 'Using interpretation outputs as alternative input data' (median Wasserstein 0.0 / 0.049)

findings.title: res/ result files that drive all figures and tables are not shipped
findings.quote:
Result files the notebook (notebooks/plots_and_tables.ipynb) reads:
  MISSING: res/all_experiments_results.parquet
  MISSING: res/all_experiments_embeddings.npz
  MISSING: res/all_experiments_embeddings_input.npz
findings.claim: notebooks/plots_and_tables.ipynb reads res/all_experiments_results.parquet and two .npz files (load_data/load_plot_data) to produce every figure and table, but none of these files (nor the res/ directory) are present in the repo; no .parquet ships at all.
findings.concern: All reported figures/tables can only be regenerated by re-running the full train -> preprocess -> explore -> group_results pipeline (which also needs GPU and HF model downloads), so none of the headline numbers are directly verifiable from shipped artefacts.
findings.resolution: Authors: ship the all_experiments_* result bundle (or a small sample) so the notebook's figures/tables can be regenerated without the full re-run, or document the exact commands and runtime to regenerate res/.

findings.title: Trained ViT checkpoints absent; only config.json + metrics.json shipped
findings.claim: models/cifar10_experiment/ and models/mnist_experiment/ contain only config.json and metrics.json (training accuracy curves); no model weight file (.pt/.pth/.safetensors) is present anywhere in the repo, so the exact ViTs used must be retrained via experiments/models/train_vit.py.
findings.concern: Exact reproduction of the reported numbers requires retraining the ViTs (nondeterministic), and the BERT models are pulled from external HF hubs; no checkpoint is pinned.
findings.resolution: Authors: release the trained ViT checkpoints (or pin exact seeds/commands), and pin the exact HF model revisions used for BERT.
findings.paper_ref: Section 4 (one ViT trained per image dataset; pretrained BERT for text)

findings.title: Paper claims all volume-ratio p-values < 1e-3, but shipped table has p = 0.007
findings.claim: The repo's own tables/volume_ratio.csv (produced by notebook cell 39) reports the hatespeech, delta=10 volume-ratio test with p = 0.007, whereas the paper states 'all p-values resulted lower than 10^-3'.
findings.concern: One of the eight reported tests does not meet the significance threshold the paper claims for all of them, so the blanket significance statement is not supported by the released results.
findings.resolution: Authors: correct the claim to reflect the actual p-values (one is 7e-3, not < 1e-3), or clarify whether a different test/aggregation produced the reported < 1e-3.
findings.paper_ref: Section 4: 'We validated these results by performing Welch t-tests on rho_V: all p-values resulted lower than 10^-3.'

findings.title: Paper says 'Welch t-tests'; code runs a one-sample t-test (ttest_1samp)
findings.quote:
"    volume_ratio_test=(\"svd_volume_ratio\", lambda x: ttest_1samp(x, popmean = 1, alternative = \"greater\").statistic),\n",
"    volume_ratio_test_pvalue=(\"svd_volume_ratio\", lambda x: ttest_1samp(x, popmean = 1, alternative = \"greater\").pvalue)\n",
findings.claim: The volume-ratio significance test is implemented as a one-sample t-test of the per-input SiMExp/SiMEC volume ratios against popmean=1 (scipy ttest_1samp), not a Welch (two-sample, unequal-variance) t-test as the paper states.
findings.concern: The named test (Welch) and the implemented test (one-sample against a fixed mean of 1) are different procedures; the one-sample test is itself a valid way to test ratio>1, so the issue is the paper's description, not the validity of the code.
findings.resolution: Authors: rename the test in the paper to a one-sample t-test (H0: mean ratio <= 1), or, if a Welch test was intended, provide that implementation and its p-values.
